segments.conditions: Segregated coaches applied through Virginia and into Washington. Many Black migrants from the Carolinas and Georgia connected through Richmond or Norfolk, having already traveled hours on regional lines to reach the PRR corridor.

North of Washington, segregation laws no longer applied and passengers could move freely between cars. The train passed through Baltimore and Wilmington, major destinations in their own right for migrants seeking industrial work in steel mills and shipyards.

The final stretch of the eastern corridor passed through the industrial sprawl of New Jersey before plunging under the Hudson River through the tunnels into Penn Station. For immigrants who had arrived at Ellis Island days or weeks earlier, this same route connected them to jobs in Philadelphia's textile mills and Baltimore's factories.

The westbound route crossed the Allegheny Mountains through the famous Horseshoe Curve, a feat of engineering that slowed travel considerably. Pittsburgh-bound migrants — both Black southerners and SE European immigrants — were heading to the steel mills that defined the city's economy and its brutal working conditions.

who_used_it: Great Migration eastern stream from Virginia, the Carolinas, South Carolina, and Georgia heading to Philadelphia, New York, and Pittsburgh. Also SE European immigrants (Poles, Italians, Hungarians, Slovaks) connecting from New York ports to Pittsburgh-area steel towns, and Jewish immigrants heading from New York to Philadelphia.
narrative_hook: In {year}, {ancestor_name} likely traveled the Pennsylvania Railroad corridor from {origin} to {destination}, following the same eastern route that carried hundreds of thousands of migrants — both Black southerners and European immigrants — into the industrial cities of the mid-Atlantic.
source_references: Albert J. Churella, 'The Pennsylvania Railroad, Volume 1: Building an Empire, 1846-1917' (University of Pennsylvania Press, 2012)
Isabel Wilkerson, 'The Warmth of Other Suns: The Epic Story of America's Great Migration' (Random House, 2010)
Joe William Trotter Jr., 'The Great Migration in Historical Perspective' (Indiana University Press, 1991)
John P. Hoerr, 'And the Wolf Finally Came: The Decline of the American Steel Industry' (University of Pittsburgh Press, 1988)
